stem:
A 55-year-old woman with a recent diagnosis of severe major depressive disorder has been an inpatient for five days. The nursing staff report she has become progressively withdrawn over the last 48 hours, is now mostly mute, refuses oral intake, and lies in bed with her arm held in unusual postures.

On examination she is awake with eyes open but does not speak or respond to questions. When the resident raises her arm passively it remains in the position placed for over twenty seconds and slowly returns to the bed (waxy flexibility). She intermittently mirrors the examiner's posture. She has no fever, no rigidity, no tremor, and no autonomic instability. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale score on examination is 16 (significant). She has not received any antipsychotic medication this admission and her usual antidepressants have been withheld in the last 24 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 84, blood pressure 124/78, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: creatine kinase 124 U/L (<200), full blood count and urea and electrolytes within normal limits, CT brain non-contrast no acute pathology

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence haloperidol 5 mg intramuscular and titrate the dose to clinical response over the next twenty four hours of monitoring
options.rationale: Antipsychotics, particularly typical agents, can precipitate or worsen catatonia and may also trigger neuroleptic malignant syndrome which clinically overlaps. They are not first-line in confirmed catatonia. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg intramuscular as antipsychotic monotherapy and admit to a high dependency mental health bed for observation
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotics are safer than typicals but still not first-line in catatonia. The first step is benzodiazepine challenge, then electroconvulsive therapy if no response. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 2 mg intravenously as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge and arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy if there is no response
options.rationale: Lorazepam 2 mg intravenous or intramuscular is the recommended first-line diagnostic and therapeutic challenge for catatonia; a meaningful response within 5 to 10 minutes both confirms diagnosis and is initial treatment. Non-response prompts urgent electroconvulsive therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily and increase to 100 mg over the next week with weekly review and ongoing inpatient supportive care
options.rationale: Standard antidepressant titration does not address acute catatonia; the syndrome requires immediate benzodiazepine challenge or electroconvulsive therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily for mood stabilisation and arrange MRI brain with contrast to exclude an organic cause
options.rationale: Valproate is not first-line for catatonia and MRI is not required when CT brain is unremarkable and there are no focal neurological signs. Time-critical lorazepam challenge should not be delayed. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of motoric, behavioural, and autonomic features (mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, mitgehen, mannerisms, echolalia, echopraxia) seen with mood, psychotic, and general medical conditions. Diagnosis is by Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale plus a positive lorazepam challenge (2 mg intravenous or intramuscular yielding meaningful response within minutes). Treatment is scheduled lorazepam (titrated up to around 8 to 24 mg per day) or electroconvulsive therapy for benzodiazepine non-responders or malignant catatonia.
key_points: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale screens for the syndrome
Lorazepam 2 mg IV is diagnostic and therapeutic
Avoid typical antipsychotics: risk of NMS and worsened catatonia
Electroconvulsive therapy if lorazepam fails or for malignant catatonia
Always exclude medical causes (infection, drugs, autoimmune encephalitis)
explanation.pearls: A positive lorazepam challenge in catatonia is a meaningful response within 5 to 10 minutes of a 2 mg dose; failure to respond after 4 mg IV is the trigger for urgent ECT planning.

subtopic_detail: Schizophreniform versus schizophrenia versus brief psychotic disorder
stem:
A 22-year-old university student is reviewed in early psychosis clinic ten weeks after his first presentation with paranoid delusions (he believed his housemates were planning to harm him) and second-person auditory hallucinations. His symptoms had been present for approximately six weeks before he was diagnosed and commenced on risperidone 2 mg at night, titrated to 4 mg.

He has been well since week four of treatment with complete resolution of delusions and hallucinations, has returned to lectures, and is functioning at his pre-illness baseline. He has had no manic or depressive episodes, no substance use, and no family history of psychotic illness on systematic enquiry. He asks his treating psychiatrist what his formal diagnosis is now that he has been well for over a month and his symptoms have completely resolved on treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 72, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: urine drug screen on initial presentation negative, full blood count, urea and electrolytes, liver function, thyroid function, CT brain all within normal limits

lead_in: Which is the most likely current diagnosis?

options.text: Schizophrenia because he has had persistent psychotic symptoms with treatment and is at high risk for chronic relapsing illness
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizophrenia requires symptoms (active and prodromal/residual) for at least 6 months. Total illness duration is 16 weeks here, below threshold. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder because his symptoms have settled rapidly with treatment and full functional recovery has been achieved
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires duration less than 1 month with full return to baseline. His symptoms lasted around six weeks before treatment plus four further weeks on treatment, exceeding one month. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder given the rapid response to antipsychotic treatment and complete functional restoration following the index psychotic episode
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires concurrent major mood episodes (manic or depressive) overlapping with active psychotic symptoms for the majority of the illness duration; he has had neither. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features given the rapid response, complete remission, and capacity to return immediately to his pre-illness baseline functioning
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires a manic episode at some point with associated psychotic symptoms. He has had no documented mood elevation or major depressive episode. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder because his total symptom duration including the prodrome falls between one month and six months from index symptoms
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizophreniform disorder requires symptoms for 1 to 6 months. His total duration of approximately 10 weeks (6 weeks before plus 4 weeks of residual on treatment) sits squarely in this window; the diagnosis converts to schizophrenia if symptoms persist beyond 6 months. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: DSM-5 differentiates primary psychotic disorders by duration: brief psychotic disorder is less than 1 month with full return to baseline; schizophreniform disorder is 1 to 6 months; schizophrenia is 6 months or more. Schizoaffective disorder requires concurrent major mood episodes overlapping with active psychosis for at least half the illness duration. Bipolar I with psychotic features requires a manic episode. About one third of schizophreniform diagnoses convert to schizophrenia within six months of follow-up.
key_points: Brief psychotic disorder: < 1 month, full return to baseline
Schizophreniform: 1-6 months total duration
Schizophrenia: >= 6 months total duration
Schizoaffective: major mood episode + psychosis concurrent
Antipsychotic taper trial after first-episode psychosis: not before 12 months symptom-free in Australian practice
explanation.pearls: Schizophreniform disorder is provisional - around one third convert to schizophrenia by six months and two thirds remit; the diagnosis is reviewed after the six month mark.

stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with three weeks of low mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia (around eleven hours nightly), increased appetite, and a four kilogram weight gain. She denies suicidal ideation. She works as a teacher and has had to take leave.

Her psychiatric history includes an episode at age 24 where she did not sleep for two weeks, believed she was destined to start a global business empire, spent twenty thousand dollars on impulse, and was hospitalised for a fortnight. She has not been on regular psychiatric medication since then because she felt well. She has no other medical comorbidities and takes a multivitamin only. She does not use alcohol or illicit substances and her thyroid function and full blood count today are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 70, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: thyroid stimulating hormone 2.1 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), free thyroxine within normal limits, full blood count and electrolytes within normal limits, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Commence quetiapine extended release 50 mg at night and titrate to 300 mg over two weeks with general practitioner review at one and two weeks
options.rationale: Her past episode (sustained two-week mood elevation with grandiosity, decreased sleep need, and impulsivity requiring hospitalisation) was a manic episode, making her current presentation bipolar I depression. Quetiapine has the strongest evidence as first-line monotherapy for bipolar depression per RANZCP. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily and titrate to 100 mg as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor monotherapy with weekly review for the first month
options.rationale: SSRI monotherapy in bipolar depression risks switch to mania or rapid cycling and is contraindicated as first-line. Mood stabiliser cover is required. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg daily as a serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor with general practitioner review at four weeks and at twelve weeks
options.rationale: Venlafaxine carries the highest switch-to-mania risk among antidepressants in bipolar disorder and should be avoided as monotherapy. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg at night for sedation and mood with titration to 75 mg over the next four weeks of close clinical review
options.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants are second-line in unipolar depression and carry significant switch risk in bipolar disorder. They are not first-line for bipolar depression. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Commence electroconvulsive therapy after four trial sessions of cognitive behavioural therapy with concurrent regular general practitioner follow up
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for severe, psychotic, melancholic, treatment-resistant, or actively suicidal depression. First-line pharmacotherapy is offered first. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Bipolar depression is differentiated from unipolar major depression by a personal history of mania (bipolar I) or hypomania (bipolar II). Antidepressant monotherapy risks switch to mania, rapid cycling, and worsening course; mood stabiliser cover is essential. RANZCP first-line agents for bipolar depression are quetiapine, lurasidone, lithium, and lamotrigine. Olanzapine-fluoxetine combination is also evidence-based.
key_points: Past mania = bipolar I; past hypomania = bipolar II
Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar depression: risk of switch and rapid cycling
First-line: quetiapine, lurasidone, lithium, lamotrigine
Olanzapine-fluoxetine is evidence-based combination
Always screen unipolar-appearing depression for past hypomania/mania
explanation.pearls: Atypical depressive features (hypersomnia, weight gain, leaden paralysis) plus a past episode of decreased sleep need with grandiosity is bipolar I depression and quetiapine has the strongest RANZCP evidence as monotherapy.

stem:
A 27-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia has been treated with risperidone 4 mg daily for eight months with good control of psychotic symptoms and no other neuropsychiatric side effects. She has been adherent.

She now reports six months of amenorrhoea and bilateral non-bloody nipple discharge for the past three months. Pregnancy test is negative. She has no headaches or visual field changes and her body weight is stable. She is otherwise well and her psychotic symptoms remain in remission. Thyroid function and prolactin have been measured today. She has no family history of pituitary disease, no past oral contraceptive use in the last three years, and no other medications including no herbal supplements or recreational substances reported on direct questioning.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 72, blood pressure 118/76, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: prolactin 1850 mU/L (60-500), thyroid stimulating hormone 2.4 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), beta-hCG negative, urea and electrolytes within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue risperidone at the current dose and add cabergoline 0.5 mg twice weekly to lower the prolactin and restore menstruation reliably
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists in patients with psychotic disorders can precipitate relapse of psychosis through dopaminergic stimulation; they are not first-line for antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Switch from risperidone to aripiprazole with a 2 to 4 week cross-titration and recheck prolactin and menstruation at six and twelve weeks of treatment
options.rationale: Risperidone causes the highest rate of hyperprolactinaemia among second-generation antipsychotics. Switching to a prolactin-sparing or prolactin-lowering agent (aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine) is first-line, with aripiprazole the most effective at normalising prolactin. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Continue risperidone, reduce dose to 2 mg daily, and add oral oestradiol valerate 2 mg with norethisterone for hormone replacement therapy
options.rationale: Hormone replacement does not address the underlying cause and combined oestrogen carries thrombotic risk. Switching antipsychotic addresses the root mechanism. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Arrange MRI of the pituitary urgently to exclude a prolactinoma before considering changes to her risperidone or any additional medical management
options.rationale: When the prolactin elevation is plausibly attributable to a known dopamine antagonist, the standard step is antipsychotic switch first; MRI is reserved for prolactin above 4000 mU/L, persistence after switch, or focal neurology. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

options.text: Continue risperidone unchanged and add depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg every twelve weeks for cycle suppression and bone health protection
options.rationale: Progestogen-only contraception suppresses menstruation without addressing prolactin or restoring physiological oestrogen, leaving bone density at risk. (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia results from dopamine D2 antagonism in the tuberoinfundibular pathway. Risperidone, paliperidone, amisulpride, and the typical antipsychotics are the highest-risk agents. Clinical consequences include amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, sexual dysfunction, reduced bone mineral density, and possibly increased breast cancer risk. First-line management is switching to a prolactin-sparing agent (aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine), with aripiprazole most effective at normalising prolactin.
key_points: High prolactin risk: risperidone, paliperidone, amisulpride, typical antipsychotics
Low prolactin impact: aripiprazole (often lowers prolactin), quetiapine, olanzapine
Switch antipsychotic is first-line management
MRI pituitary only if prolactin > 4000 mU/L, persistent after switch, or neurology
Dopamine agonists (cabergoline) risk psychotic relapse
explanation.pearls: Aripiprazole is a partial D2 agonist and can paradoxically lower prolactin below baseline, making it both the preferred switch and a possible adjunct for women who must remain on a high-prolactin antipsychotic.

stem:
A 30-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder attends a preconception planning appointment with her psychiatrist. She has been well for five years on lithium carbonate 1000 mg daily (level 0.7 mmol/L) after two severe manic episodes prior to commencing lithium, including one with psychotic features that required involuntary admission for six weeks.

She has tried sodium valproate previously which was ceased due to weight gain and unacceptable cognitive blunting, and a brief trial of lamotrigine alone was followed by an early manic episode within four months. She and her partner want to conceive within the next six months. She has no other medical comorbidities. Her renal function and thyroid function are normal. She understands the principles of medication monitoring and is willing to attend frequent specialist review during pregnancy if recommended.

data_table.Investigations: creatinine 68 micromol/L (45-90), eGFR over 90 mL/min/1.73 m squared, thyroid stimulating hormone 1.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), lithium level 0.7 mmol/L (0.6-0.8)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Continue lithium throughout pregnancy at the current once daily dose with the same monitoring schedule used outside of pregnancy
options.rationale: Lithium clearance increases by 30 to 50 percent during pregnancy, so dose adjustment and more frequent level monitoring are required. Once daily dosing also produces higher peaks linked to teratogenicity risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Stop lithium immediately for the preconception period and reintroduce it only after delivery once breastfeeding has been weaned at six months postpartum
options.rationale: Lithium cessation in a woman with severe bipolar I disorder and two prior manic episodes (one with psychosis) carries very high relapse risk during pregnancy and postpartum, with risks to maternal and foetal wellbeing exceeding the absolute teratogenic risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Continue lithium across pregnancy in divided doses with foetal cardiac ultrasound at 18 to 20 weeks and lithium level monitoring every 2 to 4 weeks
options.rationale: Severe bipolar I with documented lithium responsiveness and failed alternatives justifies lithium continuation. Risk mitigation includes TID dosing to flatten peaks, foetal cardiac ultrasound at 18 to 20 weeks for Ebstein anomaly screening (absolute risk around 1 in 1000), and lithium monitoring every 2 to 4 weeks (more frequent in third trimester) with peripartum dose reduction. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Switch lithium to sodium valproate as a first-line mood stabiliser in pregnancy planning starting now to allow for blood level stabilisation over six months
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic mood stabiliser (foetal valproate syndrome, neural tube defects up to 10 percent, and lasting neurodevelopmental impairment) and is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential without a pregnancy prevention program. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Switch lithium to lamotrigine 200 mg daily as first-line mood stabiliser through pregnancy and continue this single agent throughout the postnatal period
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is reasonable for bipolar depression maintenance but has weaker evidence for mania prevention. This patient already had a manic relapse on lamotrigine alone; switching now is not safer than continuing lithium. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Lithium use in pregnancy involves balancing absolute teratogenic risk (Ebstein anomaly around 1 in 1000, around 10 to 20 times baseline but absolute risk low) against high relapse risk if discontinued in severe bipolar I disorder. RANZCP guidance supports continuation in women with severe disease and documented lithium responsiveness, with TID dosing to flatten peaks, foetal cardiac ultrasound at 18 to 20 weeks, lithium monitoring every 2 to 4 weeks with adjustment for rising clearance, and a planned peripartum dose reduction. Valproate is contraindicated.
key_points: Lithium teratogenicity: Ebstein anomaly absolute risk around 1 in 1000
Lithium clearance rises 30-50 percent in pregnancy; dose adjustment needed
TID dosing flattens peaks and may reduce teratogenicity
Foetal cardiac ultrasound at 18-20 weeks screens for Ebstein
Valproate is contraindicated; lamotrigine has weak mania prevention evidence
explanation.pearls: Continuing lithium in severe bipolar I disorder during pregnancy is supported because the absolute Ebstein anomaly risk (around 1 in 1000) is much smaller than the relapse-driven risk to mother and baby of stopping; switch to TID dosing to flatten peaks.

subtopic_detail: Acute hyperkalaemia in chronic kidney disease
stem:
A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease stage 4 (baseline eGFR 22) presents to a metropolitan emergency department with one day of generalised weakness and palpitations. His regular medications include ramipril 10 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg at night.

On examination he is alert with proximal muscle weakness limiting hip flexion. He is normotensive and not in fluid overload. ECG shows tall peaked T waves throughout the praecordial leads without QRS widening or P wave loss. Cardiac monitor confirms sinus rhythm. He has not had any vomiting, no recent gastroenteritis, no recent radiocontrast, and no over-the-counter medication changes. He took his usual morning dose of medications. He has no history of cardiac arrhythmia and no implanted device.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 78, blood pressure 142/86, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees
data_table.Investigations: potassium 7.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), sodium 136 mmol/L (135-145), bicarbonate 18 mmol/L (22-28), creatinine 280 micromol/L (60-110), urea 22 mmol/L (3-8), eGFR 18 mL/min/1.73 m squared, glucose 8.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate sequence of management?

options.text: 10 percent calcium chloride 10 mL intravenously over 10 minutes then repeat urea and electrolytes at 30 minutes for trend assessment
options.rationale: Calcium chloride is more vesicant than calcium gluconate; gluconate is preferred for peripheral administration. Calcium also protects the myocardium but does not lower potassium, so further intracellular shift agents are required. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g orally and recheck the serum potassium in 4 hours for trend before any further intervention
options.rationale: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate has slow onset (hours) and limited evidence; it is not appropriate as monotherapy in severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Soluble insulin 10 units intravenously with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose intravenously over 30 minutes and observe for hypoglycaemia thereafter
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is essential to shift potassium intracellularly but does not address myocardial protection or remove potassium. Calcium gluconate is given first for myocardial stabilisation. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10 percent intravenously over 2 minutes, then insulin-dextrose and salbutamol nebuliser, and cease ramipril and spironolactone
options.rationale: Severe hyperkalaemia (potassium 6.5 or more) with ECG changes requires immediate calcium gluconate for myocardial stabilisation, then potassium-shifting therapy (insulin-dextrose and salbutamol nebuliser), and removal of offending drugs (ACE inhibitor and potassium-sparing diuretic). Definitive removal is via diuresis, resin, or dialysis. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol intravenously over 5 minutes and arrange urgent haemodialysis with the on-call renal team for definitive treatment
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate has modest potassium-lowering effect and is reserved for severe metabolic acidosis. Haemodialysis is definitive but is preceded by myocardial stabilisation and pharmacological potassium shift. (Source: KHA Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia (potassium 6.5 or more) or any hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is treated in the order: calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10 percent intravenous over 2 minutes for myocardial stabilisation, insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose for intracellular shift, salbutamol 10 to 20 mg nebulised as additional shift, removal of offending drugs (ACE inhibitor, ARB, potassium-sparing diuretic, NSAID), and definitive removal via diuresis, gut binders, or dialysis.
key_points: Severe hyperkalaemia: K >= 6.5 or any K with ECG changes
Sequence: stabilise myocardium, shift intracellular, then remove
Calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% IV over 2 min (peripheral preferred)
Insulin 10 units + 50 mL 50% dextrose IV; salbutamol 10-20 mg nebulised
Stop ACE inhibitor, ARB, potassium-sparing diuretic, NSAID
explanation.pearls: Calcium gluconate stabilises the myocardium within 2 to 3 minutes of administration and lasts 30 to 60 minutes, but does not lower potassium - the shift and removal steps must still follow.
